title: (Quasi)-Periods Functions and Derivatives of Period Maps
speaker: Jacob Tsimerman
abstract: Given a family X→S, one may consider the corresponding fiber-wise (quasi-) period integrals as (multi)-functions on S. Built out of these using a flag variety, one obtains variation of (mixed) hodge structures giving period map S→D/Γ. We study the question of whether one can recover the periods themselves using the period maps by taking derivatives. Specifically, we show that this is usually true (up to an algebraic closure) and explain when it fails. The proofs make use of o-minimality results. Joint work with Bakker and Pila.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Period and quasi-period fields from comparison
proof_stages.summary: Periods are fixed as coefficients of de Rham-Betti comparison matrices for varieties and, in families, as local functions from relative de Rham bundles and local systems, with quasi-periods interpreted through integrals of suitable differential forms on open complements.

proof_stages.label: Period-map functions and differential closure
proof_stages.summary: A polarized variation of Hodge structure gives a period map whose pullbacks of rational functions generate a field C(Phi), and the closure of this field under all base derivations is introduced because the full period field is stable under Gauss-Manin differentiation.

proof_stages.label: Geometric recovery theorem and unitary obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The main Hodge-theoretic theorem says C_delta(Phi) is contained in the period field and has the same algebraic closure exactly when the local system has no infinite-monodromy unitary direct summand, since such summands are invisible to the period map while their periods may remain transcendental.

proof_stages.label: Flat-bundle reformulation and algebraic monodromy
proof_stages.summary: The geometric family is replaced by an arbitrary flat bundle V with local system L, period field K_V, and algebraic monodromy group G given by the identity component of the Zariski closure of monodromy.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy lower bound for period transcendence
proof_stages.summary: A differential-Galois/Riemann-Hilbert argument shows tr.deg_K K_V is at least dim G and equals dim G for regular singular connections: the leaf in Hom(V,V_s) recording parallel transport has Zariski closure invariant under G, and regularity/definability makes the relevant G-torsor algebraic.

proof_stages.label: Definable generalized period maps
proof_stages.summary: The period-map input is abstracted to a definable, rho-equivariant map from the universal cover of the base to an algebraic G-variety Y with finite-kernel G-action, generalizing maps to compact dual period domains.

proof_stages.label: Generalized equality via algebraization and jets
proof_stages.summary: For a generalized period map, the algebraic closure of the differential field generated by pullbacks of rational functions equals the period field, because definability algebraizes monodromy-compatible functions and finite kernel allows a G-torsor to be detected in jet spaces.

examples.name: Logarithm as a prototype period function
examples.note: The logarithm is mentioned only to orient the audience toward period functions in the functional setting, not to generate any later argument.
examples.engagement_reason: Log is only named as the prototypical period function, with no computation or construction carried out on it.

examples.name: Punctured elliptic curve quasi-periods
examples.note: For an elliptic curve E and U=E minus a point, integrating a meromorphic form with a double pole supplies the missing quasi-periods, making the term 'quasi-period' concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The punctured elliptic curve is used to illustrate quasi-periods by passing from E to U=E minus a point, introducing omega and a meromorphic omega-prime with a double pole, and integrating it along cycles to get the missing periods.

passes.reason: The punctured elliptic curve quasi-periods are used to illustrate how meromorphic differentials realize quasi-periods.
A once-punctured elliptic curve is used illustratively to explain quasi-periods via meromorphic forms, not as an obstruction or structurally exceptional case.
A once-punctured elliptic curve is an ordinary open genus-one curve used to illustrate quasi-periods.
A once-punctured elliptic curve supplying meromorphic quasi-periods is a standard illustrative open elliptic curve.
A once-punctured elliptic curve is used illustratively to explain quasi-periods via meromorphic forms, with no sporadic or extremal structure invoked.
A once-punctured elliptic curve is a standard open-curve setting for quasi-period integrals, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
The once-punctured elliptic curve is used illustratively to explain quasi-periods via meromorphic forms, and is a normal open genus-one example.
Used to explain quasi-periods via meromorphic forms on an ordinary once-punctured elliptic curve.
The punctured elliptic curve is used illustratively to explain quasi-periods via meromorphic differentials, and is a standard open-curve construction rather than a structural exception.
used to illustrate quasi-periods via meromorphic differentials on an ordinary punctured elliptic curve

examples.name: Shimura varieties with real compact factors
examples.note: In response to a question, these are cited to show that unitary direct summands with infinite monodromy can occur even in semisimple variations.
examples.engagement_reason: Shimura varieties with real compact factors are invoked as an example producing unitary direct summands, without a concrete calculation or construction.

passes.reason: Shimura varieties with real compact factors give unitary infinite-monodromy summands, obstructing the naive equality statement.
Shimura varieties with real compact factors are locally symmetric examples used to show that unitary summands with infinite monodromy can occur, obstructing the naive equality statement.
A Shimura variety is locally symmetric, while a real compact factor gives a collapsed unitary summand with infinite monodromy, used to refute the idea that semisimplicity rules this out.

Shimura varieties with real compact factors are offered as an obstruction to the naive/no-unitary-summand expectation, with the compact factor giving a collapsed unitary period component in a locally symmetric setting.

examples.name: Universal elliptic curve over a modular curve
examples.note: The detailed j-function computation shows concretely that derivatives of period-map coordinates recover the period field only up to algebraic closure, so algebraic closure is genuinely needed.
examples.engagement_reason: The universal elliptic curve over a modular curve is developed in detail, including the quotient action, construction of a global form, period integrals, derivatives, and the resulting period and differential fields.

passes.reason: The modular universal elliptic curve is a highly symmetric modular/Kuga family and shows the fields need not be equal before algebraic closure.
The universal elliptic curve over a modular curve is a modular, highly symmetric family used to show that equality before algebraic closure can fail.
The universal elliptic curve over the modular curve is a modular symmetric family used to show the fields themselves need not be equal without passing to algebraic closures.
The modular universal elliptic curve is a symmetric/Kuga-type modular family used to show the fields themselves need not be equal before algebraic closure.
The universal elliptic curve over a modular curve is a modular locally symmetric family used to show the fields need not be equal before taking algebraic closure.
The universal elliptic curve is a modular homogeneous family, and its computation shows field equality can fail before passing to algebraic closure.
The universal elliptic curve over a modular curve is computed to show exact field equality fails without algebraic closure, and it is a highly regular modular/Kuga family.
Computed to show that the raw fields need not be equal, so algebraic closure is genuinely necessary; the modular-curve family is locally symmetric.
The universal elliptic curve over a modular curve is computed as a modular symmetric example that shows the fields themselves need not be equal before algebraic closure.
the modular universal family is locally symmetric and shows field equality fails before passing to algebraic closures
Shows equality before algebraic closure can fail; the universal modular elliptic family is locally symmetric and has maximal variation.

examples.name: Irregular rank-one connection q^lambda e^q on G_m
examples.note: This connection has an irregular singularity at infinity but still gives equality in the transcendence-degree bound, thereby refuting the possible converse that equality forces regular singularities.
examples.engagement_reason: The rank-one irregular connection on G_m is analyzed by specifying the solution q^lambda e^q, the connection, its irregularity at infinity, its monodromy group, and the resulting transcendence-degree equality.

passes.reason: The scalar rank-one irregular connection is a minimal-rank case used to refute equality implying regular singularities.
The rank-one connection is a minimal-rank case attaining the transcendence lower bound while being irregular, so it refutes the converse that equality would force regular singularities.
This minimal rank-one irregular connection is used to disprove the converse that equality of transcendence degree would force regular singularities.
This minimal rank-one irregular connection gives equality despite irregular singularities, refuting the converse to the regular-singular criterion.

The rank-one connection is a minimal-rank case and is used to disprove the converse that equality forces regular singularities.

Given to disprove the converse that equality forces regular singularities, and it is a minimal rank-one connection.
